stem: A hospital uses a lumbar puncture CSF analysis to screen for meningitis. The test has a sensitivity of 87% and a specificity of 80%. The prevalence of meningitis in the patient population is estimated to be between 9.6% and 16.3%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has meningitis? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(meningitis | positive test) ranges from 0.316 to 0.459 as prevalence varies from 9.6% to 16.3%. Point estimate at 13.0% prevalence: 0.394.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.2.
The confidence interval [0.316, 0.459] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (87%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
